reflection.theme: The Saturday You Couldn't Have Before
reflection.quote: The really important kind of freedom involves attention, and awareness, and discipline, and effort, and being able truly to care about other people and to sacrifice for them, over and over, in myriad petty little unsexy ways, every day.
reflection.reading:
Saturdays used to be a write-off. You either spent them recovering from Friday or building toward the next bender, and either way the day itself disappeared. There was no Saturday. There was just the wreckage between two nights.

What nobody tells you about getting sober in your twenties is that you get Saturdays back. Real ones. The kind where you wake up at 7 a.m. on purpose because you want to hit a trail before it gets hot, or because you told a friend you'd help them move, or because the coffee tastes better when you actually taste it. Wallace was right that freedom isn't the big dramatic stuff — it's the small, unsexy, daily attention. Showing up to your own life in pieces small enough that nobody but you will notice.

I used to think a sober Saturday sounded like a punishment. Eleven years in, it's the thing I'd fight hardest to keep. Not because every Saturday is a postcard — plenty of them are laundry and errands and a long walk that doesn't go anywhere. But they're mine. I remember them. They add up to a life I can describe out loud without flinching. That's the trade. Pay attention to the ordinary day, and one day you look up and the ordinary days are the life.
reflection.meditation: Today, treat the small choices like they matter — because they're what the life is made of.

affirmations: I am awake for my own Saturday, and that is not a small thing.
Today I trade the blur for the details, even the boring ones.
I am building a life I don't need to escape from.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a clear head on a weekend morning — something past-me would not have believed was possible, let alone preferable. Grateful for the friends who know the sober version of me and like him better. Grateful for the boring parts: the coffee, the quiet, the plans I'll actually remember tomorrow.
